sentences.text: How long do you have to speak?
Just--
My name is Magda Weiss.
My name-- maiden name was Magda Volmond.
I was born in Hungary a city called Gyor.
I was born in 1926 the [? 30 of days of July. ?]
My name-- my parent's name was Armin Volmond [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Irma Volmond.
Yes.
There was five of us in the family.
I have two brothers and two sisters.
My sister, the first one was Teresa.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next was Ilona.
And my brother names was Josef and Karoj.
My father have a business.
He was a kind of a steel workers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he used to work in constructing in houses and machinery.
He also have a shop something like we call here in a hardware shop.
It was the second largest town in Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Population was maybe 60,000.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a beautiful town and a lot of life in it.
My father is a man-- it's very hard to [LAUGHS] really say.
But he was a very lively person.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Good education.
He talked quite a few languages and a good provider.
Sometime he was strict, but he really like his family and gave us everything he has.
He used to take us for swimming, kayaking.
And sometime he took us to Budapest when he got the money, everyone of their own.
And he gave us quite few little treats.
But most of it, he wanted to educated us the best way he can.
He teach us with some music.
He liked music himself.
And all together, he was not too bad.
[LAUGHS]
Mother.
[LAUGHS] Mother was a childish person, but she was a very, very charming person.
Her life was more like again for music, dancing.
She was an outgoing person.
She ever liked to go to parties.
Have a lot of friends.
She also liked to play cards.
She have a little group weekly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She took her daughters every night out.
We used to have a park where people used to come together.
You show your daughters.
There was, you know, boys there.
They met with their companies.
Especially in the summertime, from there for you go maybe a little treat, cup of coffee, piece of cake.
And there was always music in the park.
They was allowed to stay out till 10 o'clock.
After 10 o'clock, you have to go home.
The town is become quiet, nighttime come.
And it was pretty well the rule.
When you go home, you met with boys there.
They take you home.
And you only call them inside when you going seriously with some boy who probably will marrying you.
And we also have a lot of other companies in the house.
The house was never empty.
Filled with people and animals, whatever you please.
[LAUGHS]
Only my grandmother and grandfather was there, also, my uncle, a brother of my mother.
My grandmother name was Josefin.
And my grandfather name was Farkas.
My father's parents.
My uncle name was Bela Pollak.
My grandparents.
My grandparents originally came from Czechoslovakia where my grandmother born.
My grandfather born probably in Poland.
And they did not live in their young life in Gyor.
They was living in a smaller place more like a village.
They have there a shop, like a grocery shop.
Plus, my grandfather have a business.
He was finishing furs and skins.
But later on when he become a bit older, they moved into the city.
Oh, grandmother.
Grandmother was another happy-go-lucky person.
She was an embroidery teacher.
And she was a very proud person on her trade.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she teached us anything she could very, very particular.
You have to be perfect or nothing.
Nothing could go in a half way.
She was very close to my father, because she have three childrens.
One was daughter, one was lost in the war.
She have an only one daughter who has an accident, and she wasn't in life.
Therefore, I was so close to her, because I take the place of her daughter.
So she practically brought us up.
And my grandmother really took me day by day with my hand and gave us all the treats she can, spoiled us.
That's the First War.
Yes.
My grandparents was very, very religious.
He gone every morning in the synagogue, never miss a day even if it's snowing or whatever.
And he was a known member of the Jewish community.
He practically was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] almost as well educated as the rabbi in cheder.
And he kept this.
And he lived for his religion.
Without his praying, the day didn't start and didn't end.
My parents was-- my mother was quite religious.
And she kept a kosher house.
My father was a deep, deep religious person.
But he wasn't as religious later on.
He kind of lost something of his religion after the war.
This was the First War.
We speak mostly Hungarian or German.
My grandparents speak more Yiddish than in German, but they also speak German.
When I was a child, mostly my friend was Jewish.
Very good.
My parents has many non-Jewish friend and close friend, too-- neighbors.
And if we need help-- say, babysitting or anyway if mother maybe go to the market-- any neighbors, Jewish or non-Jewish come and babysit for her.
And later on, we entertain each other of non-Jewish peoples.
School.
My school started also when I was 6 years old in the Jewish elementary school for four years.
And after this, there is a middle class school which is mixed.
This time it's a government school.
And I've having another four years on this school.
More like what you learn in the public school.
But then we have the Jewish arts after the time, which was the morning till 1 o'clock, we learned elementary studies.
And after 1 o'clock, the girls was also learning to reading and Hebrew.
And the boys usually stay like studying more than the girls to get ready for bar mitzvah or, you know, go to cheder.
No.
We was treated the same way.
We also have Jewish teachers in the mixed school, but only twice a week for one hour.
And all the other religion have the very same.
My favorite subject was-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] really the school, we could-- the hour when we could-- I can't find though the English word on this.
Reading algebra, math, I was a good little student.
What was my favorite?
Exercising was maybe my favorite.
[LAUGHS]
Gymnastics.
I really wanted to be a doctor.
And I would be a good one if they would allow me to go farther in the school.
But this time, there was a Jewish law against Jewish student.
And they only take you in if you are a very privileged student.
Your parents maybe was decorated in the war over and over.
They only take, I think, to go higher, about 2% of Jewish.
This was about-- started pretty well at '42.
Well, my first really well memories when my father take me to Budapest.
We have relative there.
And he took me to the zoo in Budapest and other places.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] really nothing special, because we have a very, very good time every Sunday.
We usually go to the island with the family.
Mom make a very good dinner and we eat out there.
We usually have breaded chicken, salad, and a big company with neighbors and friends.
And this was a Sunday and Saturday enjoyment.
Though, Saturday we didn't take this many food.
But we come together with friends, neighbors, boys, girls.
We was allowed to go swimming, playing, and have a good time weekly.
I was very close to my sister, the one who is still in here.
Because she's older than me.
And she was a kind of a motherly sister.
Teresa.
The next sister after, she was also motherly.
But she have her own way.
She liked to read every day.
She was a great leader.
We loved each other, but I wasn't as close.
My other brother, the one who died, he was very, very close.
His name was Karoj.
Well, he got some money.
He bring us treat.
He took me out for the bicycle riding.
He also have bigger friend, whom I have some idea I was somebody.
[LAUGHS] Because the boys maybe look at me someday.
And he took me to movies, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also to concert, and places.
My favorite movie?
What was this movie we like?
Al Jolson who was in a big one in Hungary when I could see it.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was also the comical movies, which was interesting.
Another I remember was in Hungary Mickey Rooney, and Judy Garland, and these type of a movies.
Life begin to change [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at '42.
Well, people start to talk against Jewish, the Jewish, the one who has all the money.
And they robbing people.
They're not working for this.
They are the doctors.
They are the merchant.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all together, they not working for what they have.
And then they make the other people work for next to nothing if they working for you.
And this how it started.
I was just about finished school by then.
Yes.
Well, when I was finished school, Jewish people cannot find a job anymore.
Because they only allow to take, say, if a company have 10 person, you just can't find job anymore.
Because you are Jewish.
And yet, my parents want to give me something in my hand to study.
And then finally, they find someone.
And they decide to give me a trade of something.
So they thought maybe hairdressing would be a good thing so I started.
No.
[LAUGHS] I did not like it.
I was doing it for a year and half.
Yes, and we already felt it.
Well, it's this when the anti-Semitism started and everybody becomes so a big mouth.
And they look at you down.
They maybe tell you, you are Jewish.
And you're no good.
You killed Christ and things like this.
My father thought it could never really happen what did happen.
And he couldn't go any farther.
And we believed him.
We was the type of family what father know best.
And we never thought.
But later on, when the war started in Poland and they going on and on, we really was scared to this come to Hungary.
And finally, it did came to Hungary.
We was celebrating Pesach.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was the 13 days of March.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's after this, things start to happen very, very fast.
Well, what happened?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] About four, even three weeks later, they start to gathering the Jews.
Well, the Hungarian who was working together with the Germans.
They gathered together mostly the men.
And you have to wear a yellow star.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the young boys who was taking to labor, you know.
Because they not allowed to go to soldiers.
They have to be working the hardest way.
They got very little food.
And they tortured them.
And they also start to take the mens away.
Most of the women was the one who was staying home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh.
And about five weeks later, when the German was in there, when they took my grandmother to the jail.
She was the first one to be tooked, because they claim [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when she was shopping, she is hiding her yellow star.
Which she did not, because she was wearing a scarf.
And what did she know about it anyway?
They took her to the jail.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we kind of want to hide this from our grandfather, because it would just killed him.
So we told him grandmother is visiting a relative in another city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But one day, he come to me.
And he said, you are the only one who tell me what people are talking.
And if you say it's true, it's true.
But if you say that's not true, then it's not true.
I believe you.
Is your grandmother in jail?
I lied to him.
And I said, no.
And we have to take her food.
And I was the one, because the boys wasn't home who take her food as much we have daily.
But they didn't hurt me when I go to take her food to the jail.
Oh.
She was the first.
The next was my father who was taken.
Yes.
Until-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until they take us to the barracks.
She was there for four weeks to five weeks.
I never see her.
I just was allowed to give her the food.
Not her, but the guard.
But I never had allowed to see her.
They just said, whom you bringing this food?
And they pretty well know you, because it wasn't this big city.
So they still know whom you're bringing the food, and they take it.
And--
There was bombing the city.
The first time, the American bomb the city.
They did quite a bit of damage.
In our city, there was quite few factories.
So they bombing the factory district.
And it was quite few dead and wounded.
And three days later, they took my father.
They saying, he was one of it who brought in the airplanes and the bombing and the bombers.
And they then took him to jails.
About two weeks later, we was allowed to see him.
And again, I was the one, because only one can see it.
And I don't want my mother go there, because I figured maybe she did not come home.
So I was the one who visited Father.
And I do not recognize him.
Because he's tooth was broken off.
And he was looking just like a half-dead man.
I ask him, how badly that he was beaten.
And he said, they did not over do it.
And he got quite a bit of money with him when they took him.
And we was very poor, but they gave us this money.
It was about $1,000 which-- not dollars but 1,000 pengo, which was probably his monthly taking or something.
So--
Yes.
